FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes data from U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with 1985 adolescents from Year 2-4 waves

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with prospective models examining prospective associations between sleep, social media use, and brain activation over two years

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of sleep research

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (qualifying SM variable); paired with sleep duration (Munich Chronotype Questionnaire) and brain activation (fMRI reward task); all are individual-trait-level outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective models report multiple numeric results: greater SM use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep at Year 4; effects moderated by nucleus accumbens, cingulate, insula, putamen activation; longer sleep at Year 2 predicted higher caudate activation at Year 4

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantifications of SM-outcome associations reported: direct effects (SM→sleep duration), moderation effects by neural activation regions

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal but SM appears on both sides of the equation (SM predicts sleep; sleep predicts SM), meeting bidirectional criterion; FT-EC7 requires SM-as-DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere
notes: Paper demonstrates reciprocal associations: greater SM use predicts shorter sleep; shorter sleep predicts increased SM use; effects moderated by reward/executive control brain activation

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study with N=11,878 children ages 9-10

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis estimating prevalence of emotion dysregulation and examining demographic and clinical correlates

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology

FT_IC2.evidence: No PDF available; abstract mentions "screen time use" was examined as a correlate of emotion dysregulation but does not specify whether screen time is phone-specific, SM-specific, or a multi-device composite (TV+console+phone). Cannot determine from abstract alone whether construct isolates phone/SM use per §2.4 criteria

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 returned UNKNOWN with no PDF; downstream cascade deferred
notes: Paper focuses on emotion dysregulation prevalence and correlates including psychiatric disorders, socioeconomic factors, sleep disturbances; screen time operationalization insufficient without PDF

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study across Years 2-4 with approximately 7260 participants

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects models and parallel mediation model examining longitudinal association between ADHD diagnosis and gaming addiction

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Frontiers in psychiatry

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures video game addiction (VGAQ scores), which is gaming-only exposure. Per §2.4 of criteria, "console/PC-gaming-only" and "unspecified-platform-gaming-only" do NOT qualify. Neither smartphone use nor social media use is measured or analyzed. Gaming addiction is distinct from phone/SM use and does not meet the qualifying smartphone/social-media exposure requirement

notes: Paper examines ADHD as predictor of gaming addiction with psychosocial mediators (peer involvement, school engagement, family conflict). No smartphone or social media variable measured; gaming is ineligible exposure per criteria
